summary: Italian Renaissance sculptor and painter, teacher of Leonardo da Vinci, Perugino, and Lorenzo di Credi. Known for his equestrian statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni, bronze David, Christ and St. Thomas for Orsanmichele, Putto with Dolphin, and the Baptism of Christ. Trained as a goldsmith. Also designed the golden ball and cross atop the Florence Cathedral lantern.

works.name: Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni
works.description: Unfinished equestrian statue completed by Alessandro Leopardi (and Lorenzo di Credi). Pedestal has three pillars on each side referencing the rumor that Colleoni had three testicles. Wikipedia

works.name: Baptism of Christ
works.description: Painting co-executed with Leonardo da Vinci. Vasari famously claimed that Leonardo's angel was so superior that Verrocchio gave up painting. Leonardo painted the angel at the left of the composition. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Vasari relates Leonardo drew an angel in The Baptism of Christ more beautifully, causing Verrocchio to give up painting
examples: Vasari claimed that Verrocchio gave up painting after this apprentice of his drew an angel in The Baptism of Christ more beautifully than any of Verrocchio's prior works
This artist painted the angel at left of the version of The Baptism of Christ executed by his teacher, Andrea del Verrocchio
Leonardo drew many pictures of tendons and muscles while studying under Andrea del Verrocchio

works.name: Christ and Saint Thomas (Orsanmichele)
works.description: Narrative bronze sculpture for a niche in Orsanmichele depicting Christ raising his right hand above Saint Thomas. Wikipedia

clues.clue: bronze sculpture for Orsanmichele depicting Christ and Saint Thomas
examples: A narrative bronze sculpture this artist created for a niche in Orsanmichele depicts Christ raising his right hand above Saint Thomas
This artist also sculpted Christ and St. Thomas for the Orsanmichele
Verrochio sculpted a bronze statue of Saint Thomas reaching towards Jesus for this building

works.name: David (bronze)
works.description: Bronze statue of David wearing a leather tunic, standing on Goliath's head. Housed in the Palazzo Vecchio (originally moved into the 'Hall of the Clock'). Leonardo may have modeled for it. Features flowers covering the chest and pseudo-Arabic script on the tunic. Wikipedia

examples: The Palazzo Vecchio houses this other artist's bronze statue of David wearing a leather tunic and standing on Goliath's head

clues.clue: Leonardo likely modeled for Verrocchio's David
examples: Leonardo likely modeled for Verrocchio's statue of David

clues.clue: flowers covering his chest and pseudo-Arabic script on his tunic
examples: Another depiction of this figure shows flowers covering his chest and pseudo-Arabic script on his tunic is by Andrea del Verrocchio

works.name: Putto with Dolphin
works.description: Bronze sculpture of a putto raising a leg in the air while holding a fork-tailed dolphin. Housed in the Palazzo Vecchio. Wikipedia

clues.clue: bronze sculpture of a putto raising a leg and holding a fork-tailed dolphin, in the Palazzo Vecchio
examples: A putto raises a leg in the air and holds a fork-tailed dolphin in a bronze sculpture in this building by Andrea del Verrocchio

comprehensive_summary:
Andrea del Verrocchio (c. 1435-1488) was an Italian Renaissance sculptor, painter, and goldsmith based in Florence. He is most frequently clued as the teacher of Leonardo da Vinci (frequency 5, giveaway), and also taught Perugino and Lorenzo di Credi. Before turning to sculpture and painting, he was trained as a goldsmith.

His most frequently clued work is the Equestrian Statue of Bartolomeo Colleoni in Venice, an unfinished equestrian statue completed after his death by Alessandro Leopardi and Lorenzo di Credi (frequency 4). The pedestal features three pillars on each long side, referencing the rumor that Colleoni had three testicles (frequency 3). Verrocchio famously destroyed the statue's head in a fit of rage, and when threatened with beheading, cleverly replied that he could return a head to a statue but not to his own body. The Frick Collection holds a related sculpture attributed to the wrong family due to a mistake over the testicle motif.

The Baptism of Christ, co-executed with Leonardo, is a key biographical clue: Vasari claimed Verrocchio gave up painting after Leonardo drew an angel more beautifully than any of Verrocchio's prior works (frequency 3 across direct and text-mention clues). Leonardo painted the angel at the left of the composition and reportedly drew tendons and muscles while studying in Verrocchio's workshop.

Verrocchio created a bronze Christ and Saint Thomas for a niche in Orsanmichele, depicting Christ raising his right hand above the doubting saint (frequency 3). His bronze David, housed in the Palazzo Vecchio, shows the young hero wearing a leather tunic with flowers on the chest and pseudo-Arabic script, standing on Goliath's head (frequency 2); Leonardo may have modeled for this statue. The Putto with Dolphin, also in the Palazzo Vecchio, depicts a putto raising a leg while holding a fork-tailed dolphin.

Other notable works and facts include: the Lady with Flowers, the first marble bust to include the subject's hands; La Piagnona, the bell used by Savonarola that was subsequently put on trial and flogged; a collaboration with Michelozzo on the Florence Baptistry's silver altar of San Giovanni; life-size wax figures of the targets of the Pazzi Conspiracy (with Orsino Benintendi); a commemorative statue of marble, porphyry, and bronze for Piero and Giovanni de Medici; the golden ball and cross designed for the top of Brunelleschi's Florence Cathedral lantern; a marble relief of Alexander the Great (cited by Andrew Butterfield as possibly the only authentic Verrocchio in the National Gallery); and an altarpiece for Pistoia Cathedral introducing the Madonna, child, and saints framed by a loggia and landscape views.
